美国的失业率仍然顽固地高达6.3％。工作增长已经停滞不前，1月份的工作比同月比同月相同，较少960万。但Gig公司表示，他们发现人们难以开车，接送和送给他们。
 “我担心一件事进入今年下半年：我们是否会有足够的司机来满足我们将要拥有的需求？” Uber Ceo Dara Khosrowshahi上个月告诉分析师。 Doordash首席财务官Prabir Adarkar称之为“一个两个城市的故事”，与新客户的部落赛车赛车订购，但司机提供的司令部更少。在2020年的最后一部分的Doordash订单超过了两倍，与同期相比前一年。
 迫在眉睫的司机短缺混淆了高管的预测。 “凭借记录失业，我们预计驾驶员供应给越来越多的骑手需求”为“可预见的未来”，Lyft Ceo Logan Green在5月份表示。在大流行早期，Lyft阻止了新的司机注册。它是可以理解的，因为今天的科技演出公司出生于巨大的经济衰退期间。他们受益于新的工人深层工人，并突然需要补充收入。
  这就是这个时间不同的原因。大流行袭击后，人们停止走动，骑行需求通过地板。司机通过离开服务来回应。新学校纽约市事务中心经济和财政政策署署长詹姆斯帕罗特已经履行了纽约市的乘车冰雹旅行的推翻和流动。他的数据表明，与2019年12月，1月份城市的乘坐总数大约一半。司机的数量也下降至82,500人。
    “司机的供应并不像曾经一样好，但对司机的需求并不像往常一样好，”斯坦福大学商业研究生院的经济学。他几乎忘记了Uber，他笑话。像大量的其他美国人一样，他没有在几个月内。司机，就像其他许多其他人一样，已经停止为健康原因工作：因为他们不想捕捉病毒或因为他们与高风险类别的人住在一起。
 关于驾驶员供应的高管之间的焦虑突出了演出经济的核心特征以及公司称之为“双面市场”。他们必须平衡供应的乘车需求。事实上，这些公司依靠司机供过于求，一支独立工人的军队，即时迅速回应客户要求，何时以及在他们所需要的地方。
 “乘车共享中最大的挑战之一正在创造市场平衡，确保您对每个骑手有足够的驾驶员，”Lyft的Green上个月表示。 “两者回应了非常不同的时间表。” 
然后有一个意想不到的因素：国家匆匆建设的大流行安全网似乎正在运作，让人们不太绝望。像大多数美国人一样，Gig工人收到了刺激检查 - 最后一个春天，1月份较小的一个。 （另一个似乎正在途中。）对于曾经拥有其他全职工作的演出工人，各国延长了失业保险金。而且，第一次，Gig工人 - 以及所有其他自由职业者 - 曾经有资格获得联邦失业保险，每周600美元的形式。
 GIG公司说他们感受到了影响。 Uber的发言人表示，该公司的数据表明，根据失业保险政策，其司机的数量可用。例如，当刺激击中驱动程序的银行账户时，较少登录到应用程序。 Adarkar，Doordash CFO，上个月告诉投资者，“一方面，您将在失业率提高时预计划线仪的大量涌入。但这是通过刺激检查的一定程度抵消。“
    JPMorgan Chase Institute于2018年出版的研究表明，家庭往往转向在线演出工作，特别是驾驶，在家庭中的某人失去工作后，需要补充他们的收入。基本上，人们在需要额外的现金时签署演出。
 研究所的新研究发现，联邦大流行援助和刺激检查计划的扩张已经给予家庭，特别是低收入家庭，比以前所做的手掌更具现金。到夏季结束时，普通家庭银行账户比以前的一年持续了40％的资金。
 “我并不感到惊讶，人们没有回到平台，因为政府已经做了一切都很好地满足了人民的需求，”研究所共和国菲奥娜格雷格说。大流行援助旨在劝阻工人在公共卫生紧急情况下寻找工作。与其他国家失业方案不同，它不要求工人在接受付款时积极寻找工作。所以现在，工人可能不需要演出。
  根据高管的评论，驾驶员供应不太可能是一个存在的问题，至少在长期以来。解决方案也很简单。 “作为一个劳动力经济学家 - 我是招聘工人困难的雇主的市场回应是支付更高的工资，”新学校的帕罗特说。有一些轶事证据表明，发生了一些城市的司机 - 包括印第安纳波利斯，俄克拉荷马城，匹兹堡和萨克拉门托，说他们已经收到了来自GIG公司的电子邮件和通知，如果他们签署并完成一些旅行。 Lyft上个月表示，在去年年底将其招聘费用减少1500万美元后，本季度将在驾驶员激励措施增加1000万美元和2000万美元。
 但是何时鼓励工人在车轮后面的问题是棘手的。公司希望有很多人签署并准备好一旦美国人接种疫苗并准备恢复正常的生活，所包括优步乘坐。 
“将更多的司机带到系统上需要时间”，“绿色，Lyft Ceo上个月表示。 “这更像是指转向和转动泰坦尼克号，而需求可以移动得多。” 不要告诉他那个是如何结束的。
note_en:
Unemployment in the US remains stubbornly high at 6.3 percent. Job growth has stalled, with 9.6 million fewer jobs in January than the same month a year earlier. But gig companies say they’re having trouble finding people to drive, pick up, and deliver for them.
 “I’m worried about one thing going into the second half of the year: Are we going to have enough drivers to meet the demand that we’re going to have?”  Uber CEO Dara Khosrowshahi told an analyst last month. DoorDash chief financial officer Prabir Adarkar called the situation “a tale of two cities,” with  hordes of new customers racing to order takeout but fewer drivers offering to deliver it. DoorDash orders more than tripled in the last part of 2020, compared with the same period a year earlier.
 The looming driver shortage confounds executives’ predictions. “With record unemployment, we expect driver supply to outstrip rider demand” for the “foreseeable future,”  Lyft CEO Logan Green said in May. For a time early in the pandemic, Lyft blocked new drivers from signing up. It was understandable, because today’s tech gig companies were born during the Great Recession. They benefited from a deep pool of workers newly outfitted with smartphones and suddenly in need of supplemental income.
  Here’s why this time is different. After the pandemic hit, people stopped moving around, and demand for ride-hail went through the floor. Drivers responded by leaving the services. James Parrott, the director of economic and fiscal policies at The New School’s Center for New York City Affairs, has tracked the ebb and flow of ride-hail trips in New York City. His data suggests that the total number of rides in the city in January fell by roughly half compared with December 2019. The number of drivers fell too, to 47,600 from 82,500.
    “The supply of drivers is not as good as it used to be, but demand for drivers is really not as good as it used to be,” says Paul Oyer, who studies economics at the Stanford Graduate School of Business. He’s almost forgotten about Uber, he jokes. Like plenty of other Americans, he hasn’t been in one in months. Drivers, like so many others, have  stopped working for health reasons: because they don’t want to catch the  virus or because they live with people in high-risk categories.
 The anxiety among executives about driver supply highlights a core feature of the gig economy and what the companies call their “two-sided marketplaces.” They have to balance demand for rides with supply. In fact, the companies rely on an  oversupply of drivers, an army of independent workers standing by to respond to customer requests quickly, when and where they’re needed.
 “One of the biggest challenges in ride-sharing is creating marketplace balance, making sure you have enough drivers for every rider,” Lyft’s Green said last month. “And the two respond on very different timelines.”
  Then there’s an unexpected factor: the nation’s hastily constructed pandemic safety net appears to be working, leaving people less desperate. Like most Americans, gig workers received stimulus checks—one last spring and a smaller one in January. (Another one  appears to be on the way.) For gig workers who used to have other full-time jobs, states have extended unemployment insurance payments. And for the first time, gig workers—and all other freelancers— became eligible for a form of federal unemployment insurance, $600 a week.
 Gig companies say they’ve felt the impact. A spokesperson for Uber says the company’s data suggests that its number of drivers available fluctuated according to unemployment insurance policies. When the stimulus hit drivers’ bank accounts, for example, fewer signed in to work on the app. Adarkar, the DoorDash CFO, told investors last month that “on the one hand, you would have expected a large influx of Dashers as a result of heightened unemployment. But that was offset to some degree by stimulus checks.”
    Research from the JPMorgan Chase Institute  published in 2018 suggests that families tend to turn to online gig work, especially driving, after someone in the household loses a job and needs to supplement their income. Basically, people sign on to gig when they need extra cash.
 Newer research from the institute finds that the expansion of federal pandemic assistance and stimulus check programs have given families, especially low-income families, more cash on hand than they did before. By the end of the summer, average household bank accounts held 40 percent more money than they did the year before.
 “I’m not surprised that people aren’t returning to platforms, because the government has done a really good job of meeting peoples’ needs,” says Fiona Greig, co-president of the institute. Pandemic assistance was designed to discourage workers from looking for jobs in the middle of a public health emergency. Unlike other state unemployment programs, it doesn’t demand that workers actively look for jobs while accepting payments. So for now, workers might not need to gig.
  According to executives’ comments, driver supply isn’t likely an existential issue, at least in the long term. It’s also pretty simple to solve. “As a labor economist—which I am—the market response for employers who have difficulty recruiting workers is to pay higher wages,” says the New School’s Parrott. There’s some anecdotal evidence that that’s happening: drivers in some cities—including Indianapolis, Oklahoma City, Pittsburgh, and Sacramento, California—say they’ve received emails and notifications from gig companies offering one-time bonuses if they sign on and complete a few trips. Lyft said last month that it would spend between $10 million and $20 million more this quarter on driver incentives, after cutting its recruitment costs by $15 million at the end of last year.
 But the question of when to encourage workers to get behind the wheel is tricky. The companies want to have lots of people signed on and ready to go once more Americans get vaccinated and are ready to resume normalish lives, Uber rides included.
 “Bringing more drivers onto the system takes time,” Green, the Lyft CEO, said last month. “It’s a little more like steering and turning the  Titanic, whereas demand can move much faster.” Don’t tell him how that one ends.
title: 虽然经济衰退，但Gig公司担心工人短缺
title_en: Gig companies fear a worker shortage, despite a recession

note:
Susanne Foitzik是一名骄傲的尸体医学家：一个专门从事蚂蚁的昆虫学家（这对我来说是一个新的词汇词）。 Ludwig Maximilian大学的Ludwig Maximilian大学研究奴隶制作蚂蚁物种之间的动态，捕获其他物种的蚂蚁并让他们为他们工作，以及他们利用的宿主物种。什么遗传的变化使诸如Temnothorax Longispinosus的稀有工人蚂蚁的物种变成了诸如Temnothorax Americanus这样的奴隶制造商的蹂躏的部门？
 和什么诱导奴役的蚂蚁工作者在反叛中升起，杀死他们的压迫者的蛹？ （这不是隐喻;它真的发生了）。蚂蚁蛋和幼虫尚未制作特定物种的香味，因此奴役的维持者关心他们认为他们认为他们正在养老年轻人的殖民地。然而，虽然辣妹击中蛹阶段，他们开始像他们注定的奴隶制造者一样臭，他们的看护人意识到他们已经被欺骗了。在那一点，他们“咬了无责任的年轻昆虫，把它们撕掉，把它们从筑巢室里扔出来。”
  Foitzik博士真的，真的很喜欢蚂蚁 - 甚至是奴隶的种类。爱情闪耀着她的新书，蚂蚁帝国的每页闪耀：隐藏的世界和地球的非凡生活和奥拉夫·弗里奇·弗里奇的微小征服者。事实上，她爱他们，这是她选择的是，他们选择用她每天参与日常活动的不同蚂蚁物种的迷人图纸开始每个章节（见上文）。
 她写的是，地球上有至少16,000种已知的蚂蚁，而她没有向我们介绍每一个人，我们会遇到大量的突出。 ＆＃39; S anoplolepis Gracilipes，黄色疯狂蚂蚁，一种侵入性物种，自20世纪90年代以来，在印度洋的圣诞岛和杀死了数百万天然的红圣诞岛螃蟹。 Jaglavak是驾驶员蚂蚁，即喀麦隆北部的Morfu人民称为昆虫王子，他们倾向于战斗并驱逐渗透其住宅墙壁的任何白蚁。和Myrmelachista schumanni通过杀死任何植物，使单种“魔鬼花园”用秘鲁和巴西装饰秘鲁和巴西，除了他们培养为巢穴的主持人。
 然后有没有odontomachus bauri，它的颌骨在130百万分之一的占空比中，每小时143英里的143英里 - “在动物世界中记录的最快现象之一。”没有猎物可以逃脱。这些钳口也用于运动：当其中一个蚂蚁将她的下颚指向地面并咬伤时，斜线飞过空气。
 蚂蚁社会是精心高效和组织的;每个人都有一份没有任何问题，不满或犹豫的工作。皇后队产卵。不同的工人倾向于幼虫，建造和维护巢穴，侦察和饲养食物。士兵们守护巢的入口，找到并杀死猎物。较旧的叶形刀蚂蚁砍伐叶子，让他们回到年轻的羊皮蚂蚁，以喂养殖民地农场作为食物的真菌。
 哦，和男性？ “雄性蚂蚁不仅仅是飞捆的精子，并且到目前为止是一个完全有组织的母系状态的最乏味的蚂蚁，”Foitzik博士写道。他们从未受精卵中发展，经过一次递送精子的机会，他们死亡并被谁发现他们，往往是他们的姐妹。 
我们用人格标签表征了这些蚂蚁：侦察员，觅食者，湿护士，皇后，士兵，甚至农民和牧羊人。但正如Foitzik博士所指出的那样，蚂蚁由于遗传学和自然选择的运作而纯粹地制定了他们的指定的角色。他们很少自动化。我们有遗嘱和道德和理性推理。 Ant角色不是人类角色，蚂蚁社会不是人类社会。我们只是看到他们并以这种方式描述它们，因为我们很难想象任何其他人。
 似乎是罂粟科学书籍的趋势，因为亨丽蒂塔缺乏的不朽生活，这一人们从第一人称的角度来看，非常突出的作者。我们了解所有审判和攻击贫困博士Foitzik博士和她的同胞必须在其蚂蚁研究中，在领域和实验室。他们都肮脏;他们的研究科目咬了一口，刺痛了他们，让他们被海关代理人拘留。有时，到他们的chagrin，蚂蚁甚至出现在菜单上。
 实际研究也不容易。显然，在他们正在学习的殖民地中，这真的很难分开。并提取和解剖蚂蚁的微小大脑可以在技术上是非常具有挑战性的。这可能是令人厌恶的阅读，因为大概，MyRMecologists选择了他们的道路;没有人迫使他们在挖掘蚂蚁殖民地上爬行，把它们带回飞机上，并将它们分开。
 但蚂蚁的总体帝国提供了蚂蚁生命周期，通信和殖民地形成的巨大概述，撒上如上所述的一些六个左侧物种的真正迷人的描述。它通过概述我们的世界和社会与他们相交的方式结束。如果虫子是你的东西（是的，我知道蚂蚁不是技术上的错误），值得回避。
note_en:
Susanne Foitzik is a proud myrmecologist: an entomologist who specializes in ants (it was a new vocab word for me, too). Her lab at the Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich studies the dynamics between slave-making ant species, which capture ants of other species and get them to work for them, and the host species they exploit. What genetic changes have turned a species of diligent worker ants like   Temnothorax longispinosus  into ravaging hordes of slave makers like   Temnothorax americanus ?
 And what induces the enslaved ant workers to rise up in revolt, killing their oppressor’s pupae? (This is not metaphorical; it really happens). Ant eggs and larvae don’t yet make a species-specific scent, so the enslaved nursemaids caring for them think they’re rearing the young of their own colony. Once the babes hit the pupal stage, though, they start to stink like the slave-makers they are destined to become and their caretakers realize they’ve been duped. At that point they “bite the defenseless young insects to death, rip them to shreds, and throw them out of the nesting chamber.”
  Dr. Foitzik really, really loves ants—even the slave-making kind. That love shines through on every page of her new book,   Empire of Ants: The Hidden Worlds and Extraordinary Lives of Earth's Tiny Conquerors, co-authored with Olaf Fritsche. She loves them so much, in fact, that she's chosen to start each chapter with her charming drawings of different ant species engaged in their daily activities (see example above).
 She writes that there are at least 16,000 known species of ants on Earth, and while she doesn’t introduce us to every single one, we meet plenty of standouts. There's   Anoplolepis gracilipes , the yellow crazy ant, an invasive species that has taken over Christmas Island in the Indian Ocean and killed tens of millions of the native red Christmas Island crabs since the 1990s. Jaglavak is the driver ant that the Morfu people of Northern Cameroon refer to as the Prince of Insects and who they enlist to fight and expel any termites infiltrating the walls of their dwellings. And   Myrmelachista schumanni  make the monocultural “devil’s gardens” dotting the jungles of Peru and Brazil by killing any plant except for the single species they cultivate as hosts for their nests.
 Then there’s   Odontomachus bauri , which has mandibles that snap together in 130 millionths of a second, at 143 miles per hour—”one of the fastest phenomena recorded in the animal world.” No prey can escape. These jaws are used for movement, too: When one of these ants points her jaws down at the ground and bites, the backlash catapults her through the air.
 Ant societies are meticulously efficient and organized; every individual has a job which she performs without any question, discontent, or hesitation. The queens lay eggs. Different workers tend the larvae, build and maintain the nest, and scout out and forage food. The soldiers guard the entrance to the nest and find and kill prey. Older leafcutter ants go cut up leaves and bring them back for younger leafcutter ants to feed to the fungus that the colony farms as food.
 Oh, and the males? “Male ants are little more than flying bundles of sperm and by far the most boring ants in a perfectly organized matriarchal state,” writes Dr. Foitzik. They develop from unfertilized eggs, and after one chance of delivering their sperm, they die and are eaten by whoever finds them, often their sisters.
  We characterize these ants with human labels: scouts, foragers, wet nurses, queens, soldiers, even farmers and shepherds. But as Dr. Foitzik points out, ants enact their assigned roles purely because of the workings of genetics and natural selection. They are little automatons. We have free will, and moral and rational reasoning. Ant roles are not human roles, and ant societies are not human societies. We just see them and describe them that way because it’s hard for us to imagine any other.
 As seems to be the trend in poppy science books since   The Immortal Life of Henrietta Lacks , this one is told from a first person perspective and very prominently features the author. We hear about all the trials and travails poor Dr. Foitzik and her fellow myrmecologists must undergo in their ant studies, both in the field and in the lab. They get all dirty; their research subjects bite them, sting them, and cause them to get detained by customs agents. Sometimes, to their chagrin, the ants even show up on menus.
 The actual research isn’t easy, either. Apparently it can be really hard to tell individuals apart in the colony they’re studying. And extracting and dissecting the ants’ tiny brains can be technically quite challenging. This can be irksome reading because, presumably, the myrmecologists chose their path; no one forced them to crawl around digging up ant colonies, bring them home on planes, and cut them apart.
 But overall  Empire of Ants provides a great overview of ant life cycles, communication, and colony formation, sprinkled through with really fascinating depictions of some of the weirder species as outlined above. It ends by outlining the ways in which our world and society intersects with theirs. If bugs are your thing (and yes, I know that ants are not technically bugs), it’s worth checking out.
title: 我们生活在蚂蚁的星球上
title_en: We’re living on a planet of ants
